author: @kornshadow097
text:
So it's that simple.. What's the difference between a battery, capacitor or super capacitor? They all seem to look the same component wise... 

Also why are your cheap SC's "15x more powerful vs manufacturers "?? I know companies want profit but it seems dumb that batteries and capacitors have evolved so little after lithium ion batteries came out.. Which are always headlined for their bomb like qualities.. You'd think super capacitors would have replaced batteries by now.. Especially when LI-ION'S sought after properties are closer to a SC than a battery.. Except is an unstable version of a SC,since it's still a battery.. 

Also what components are most important? To me it seems like the active component based on the graphene craze and higher capacity... 

How do I email you? I'd like to learn and discuss things with someone intelligent who is humble :3 please.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am sorry mate I am afraid I just don't 'chat' - I get around 200 or so emails a day and I try to answer them all - that means my time per mail is limited and I already spend 2 -3 hours a day writing to folks - with all the other projects I am involved in there just isn't time to do any more - your questions are very broad and I couldn't really do them justice but there reasons behind all of them and they are far from dumb reasons as for the differences sometimes it's minimal and sometimes quite a lot but mostly it is the mechanism that is being used to store energy and the arrangement you have to use to harness that mechanism

author: @croatanjohnsontechnologies9865
text:
Hi Rob,
You stated that the active material is to be the same, would the conductive surfaces be the same also or different?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: depends on symmetric or asymmetric design

replies.author: @croatanjohnsontechnologies9865
replies.text: Thanks, this calls for an experiment to see which one would perform better. We wonder if Aluminum Sulfate on one side and Magnesium Sulfate on the other side with Naval Jelly mixed with a polymer as the electrolyte would work... We'll try to post a video response with the results.

author: @leonardjanus5538
text:
I made a solid electrolyte mixing nano-tourmaline, fumed silica and PEO (molecular weight 35000) at 90°C and then cast a film on PetriDish.
Obtained a solid electrolyte for alkaline cell that no need any salt, add only DI water.
It don't allow ion transmigration and dendrite perforation.
Have a good conductivity equal to a 20-30% KOH.
If Rob want to try, I can send him some nano-tourmaline (not Schorl).
PEO and fumed silica  is not hard to find.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i'd love to give it a go mate

author: @90aaronf
text: hi robert, i love the idea of using the kombucha scoby as a seperator for a battery or a capacitor.. i dont know a whole lot about making capacitors but i have a few kombucha scobys and would like to give  it a try. would i still need a electrolyte with the scoby to make a capacitor? if so what would you recommend as an electrolyte

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: try salt water to start with - it's cheap non toxic and you should get results - you can experiment with other things later. By the way if you grow you kombucha in graphene oxide water it will adsorb the GO and you can reduce the GO coated fibrils back to graphene coated fibrils - that would be a very very interesting electrode material - you could grow supercaps lol

replies.author: @90aaronf
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith thanks so much for all the info.. i currently dont have the means or time to make my own GO at the momebt but would be interested in purchasing some if you had some for sale, would you ship to canada?
